speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. It is July 20, 1995. We're interviewing Samuel Klasner, K-L-A-S-N-E-R, in Sunrise, Florida. We were talking about Regensburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to Regensburg, we have it, it was not a camp. It was private. So the UNRRA, and they hire us and they joined, helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Helped us. They have it in the United States, food cans, you know, like milk, canned milks, tomatoes, other kinds of things, and meat, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were displaced in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few hundred. Not too much. It's a small town. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was working in the [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I got extra pay so I could survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Private.

speaker: interviewer
text: In an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: A room.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a room. Was it in a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: A house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who owned the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. This is from the Nazis, we took away the rooms for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the German feel that Jews were living in their house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were living, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How did they feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you don't have it a family, you get just one room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they were not bad. They always say they don't know about this, everything. This is impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it's impossible?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I can think this is impossible? It was this. My life was in the sky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like on a daily basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came together. We went dancing. We went to Munich. We came back and we started a little to make business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to have it a place what you could buy alcohol. That's the 99%. With this, we make cognac. We sell to the soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a bootlegger.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sell to the soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had American money. And they always come. And from this, we maked a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was from 1945 to 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the process for you to leave? Did you have intentions to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I wanted. First of all, I want to go to Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They send our paper affidavit. I couldn't get the visa because I'm a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So my sister said I should go to Italy or to French. And reverse to be a Gentile to go to Brazil. I said no. No money in the world can pay me I should change my religion. Hitler made me for a Jew and I'm going to die a Jew. And I don't want to go. That's why we was-- an American, they were there. I don't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I make business with her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. About half an hour away to an hour from [NON-ENGLISH] to [NON-ENGLISH] was the name. So he used to buy alcohol from me because he make the cognac in [NON-ENGLISH] for the soldiers. And after I left in 1949, he died. He got an operation of the legs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he didn't survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died. He couldn't survive. So she was left with three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your wife from originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's from Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had three small children.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were. The little-- the younger daughter was, I think, five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the youngest daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betty. The-- the older one-- the young one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, the young one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen. And the middle one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the older one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their son, Eli.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eli. And where were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they lived through the war in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew your wife's husband and your wife from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And I find out when they came to German, my wife came to German. So we used to go to the boat looking who was coming. So they were. And he recognized me, the son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started to be interested. Because they no want to rent an apartment because she doesn't have the husband, so i sign it. I make that she shouldn't. In 1951, I get married. Did you have any additional children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two. He's there, the guy here, Bruce, and Joey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one's older?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joey is older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joey was born-- do you remember what year? What his birthday is?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Bruce?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you-- let's backtrack a little bit. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you first came to America, where did you come to?

speaker: interviewer
text: New Orleans. So you came from Germany to New Orleans?

speaker: interviewee
text: New Orleans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to New Orleans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they send me to Tyler, Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: From New Orleans to Tyler, Texas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long-- how long were you in New Orleans first?

speaker: interviewee
text: New Orleans was a few days because they-- over there, I went with a boat. And to go to Texas, I went with a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Texas in a couple of days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Texas? No. In Texas, I was two-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] a few months, about two or three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Texas? Why did they send you to Texas?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they have it the contract to go, to Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: What contract?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give me a contract, the Jewish-- Jewish Appeal, give me a contract and they brought me over to Texas, Tyler, Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you supposed to do in Tyler, Texas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I supposed to do-- I don't know myself. They put me in a factory. There were pipes, a factory from pipes, toilet pipes. You know, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sewer pipes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewer pipes. I was working. He was-- the owner was a Jew. And the work, it was [NON-ENGLISH]. He put me where the [NON-ENGLISH] is. I was working. I was making $16 or something. I see what's going on. I pick up myself and went to New York by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay in New York? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your wife with you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the Bronx, I rent a room. I paid $30 this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was looking for work for a carpenter. So a friend, he was working the carpenter already. He pushed me in. I have to have it. The union, if you don't have the union, they don't took you in. So I got-- I still have the card-- the book from the union house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was working as a carpenter. And this is the inside and outside. Some days, you couldn't finish a day-- hot, like rock. In winter time, it was raining, snowing, you don't have enough to work. So I work two or three days a week. Two--

speaker: interviewee
text: I married her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I change. I was working another year carpenter. I went into business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grocery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, first of all, beginning-- I was working in a store. They call them Dutch Deli. In the Bronx says [NON-ENGLISH]. And Brooklyn was Utica Avenue and Church Avenue.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working there, and then I became a manager in appetizing [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and cold cut. And I was working a few years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I went in in a coffee shop, The Shelton Tower Hotel on Lexington Avenue. I was working there. This, I bought it. You know, it was the-- the Wilford in 1964, 1965. It was standing two years and was not do so good. First of all, I was not so good in English. And they steal from me. I catch them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had it hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went out on this and I went in grocery, Italian-- Italian food with cheeses, and cold cut, and everything. I was standing until I came here in 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you've been in Sunrise since 1979?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 17 years I'm here in Sunrise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Joseph. What does he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he's old, 40 years, he's going to be this--

speaker: interviewee
text: 42 years, yeah. And I think he is in the-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I can't get it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does Bruce do? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bruce finished college. And when he came here, he supposed to continue as law. So he was late to go into college. He has to wait another year. So he start to go out and work. And he married.

speaker: interviewer
text: If there's anything that you want to tell the present generation about your experiences, what could you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they have to be careful. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And one thing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they should-- when they're young, they should go to Israel. And have more country who can fight for you, in case we need.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you want to give to your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them already. I said-- I just-- the same thing when I was talk to the high school in the other day, I told them one wish I have. You shouldn't go through what I went through. You just stay on as a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait, wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now you got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Klasner, you have a lovely family. Can you tell me who everybody is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- my wife is Lida Klasner. My son is Bruce Klasner. My daughter-in-law is Sherry Klasner. And then you got here Lenny Klasner, a granddaughter. My grandson is Eden Klasner. And my-- who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adopted grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Adopted grandson. Steven--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tepperberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tepperberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tepperberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does your son do? Do you want to tell me, Bruce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm a social worker. My wife is a school teacher. And anything else you'd like to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about your father's experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, last year, I went on the March of the Living. And I was able to see with my own eyes. I summed it up. And if my father would allow me, I wrote a poem. And it basically describes how I feel. I went to his hometown. I walked the streets that his mother was on, my namesake.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just too emotional. And it's been many months since I really dealt with the emotion part of it. But the nice thing about it is it brought me closer to my father and made me understand how much I really love him and how much he did to survive for us, more than any father could have done for a child. And that's the amazing part of the whole story. Can I read the poem? Yes, you may.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called 'The Ashes from Hell and I Will Live.' Why is this so important for us to remember, the horrors, the pain so detailed and candor? They're rounding up all of my friends. They're coming down our street again. Oh, how do I feel all so alone? Should I throw the stone?

speaker: interviewer
text: Nowhere to go or to hide in the night. It's like a candle burning with no light. The air is so thick, you could feel the fear. Is my end coming so very near? They can confine me behind these four camp walls, but they can never break my spirit. That will never fall.

speaker: interviewee
text: For my mother and father, I will stay alive. To prove a point, for I will not die. I kindle my own spirit for all of you to see. I am a Jew, and they can't take that away from me. Hold on to your hearts, for something I must teach you, things about your people that you never knew. You were told that we never fought back. Education is the only thing you lack. We fought back by getting up each and every day. You probably thought that using guns was the only way. It only took a moment to either live or to die, but [NON-ENGLISH] wasn't a big hoax or even a lie. I'm living proof that Hitler didn't win. To forget or to ignore what happened is the only sin. We are all survivors with our own stories to tell about our survival from the ashes of hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think of your grandpa?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? You like him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to give him kisses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's switch spots.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, guys, settle down, settle down. Well, I think you have a beautiful family. And I think you're a very, very lucky man, and you're a blessed man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. You don't see all of them. If you would see all of them, you would say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: We will be the king's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we have definitely the king family here, Jewish style.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish style, that's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sit still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Lida Klasner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Louder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lida Klasner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lida Klasner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- I'm going to tell you the story very short. I, you know, met my husband in Germany. I didn't marry him. My husband, my first husband, but he knew. And the boat, it was-- the ship was arriving here-- the refugees, you know, from 1950. It's, I think, Doctor-- I mean the President Truman, because of him, I'm here. When I came, when he came to see my husband, but he didn't know that he is dead. And then he took interest and we start dating. And that's what we decide, you know, he is going to stay. He wants to go to Brazil to his sister, but he couldn't because something-- because he is a Jew, you know. They wouldn't let him in. And it was my luck that he stayed here and we got married in 1951. And he said I got three children. I didn't want to have nobody. He said, look, my family all dead. I would like to have a family. If you want to get married to me, you have to give me a family. And I give him two sons. And I'm very happy. And he was very good to my children. And we got a very good life, not a rich one, but a nice life.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had a message to give to your grandchildren--

speaker: interviewee
text: To my children?

speaker: interviewer
text: To your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren. All of them?

speaker: interviewer
text: All of them. What would you like to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I would like to tell them is that I'm a lucky woman. That I have my grandchildren, and they're all nice, and they're all good. I love them, they love me. And I have a lot of joy from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to tell your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children? My children, I would like to say that they're all nice, but the special, this son what is here, Bruce, that we're more close. And he's the baby. And the baby is taking good care of us. He is very devoted. And that's what I said. The other children, they're very good, fine. If I need them, they're all there. When he went through the bypass, they all came to see him. I can have no objection, you know, that I love them all. And they love me. My all friends said, what kind of vitamin I took that I have such a beautiful family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I have to tell you that your family is filled with love. It shows. And, obviously, it's because the two you love each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I'm very happy. Really, like I said, you know, we got a very hard life. He was a very hard worker, but I didn't mind. We did everything together. And we all make the good things. We send them to school. There was good children. And that's what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is my father, Jozef Klasner--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, Lydia Klasner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- my father was taken in Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken in Philadelphia?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was here before the first war. And he wasn't in Philadelphia. So he sent his picture to my mother. So this is the two of them. You see how it looks there? The middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're put together.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're spliced together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my mother, Bella Klasner, my aunt, Fradel Rottholtz. It was taken in Wolbrom, behind the building. I took it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my sister, Rachel Klasner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this? This I have it, my sister, Lola Gilbert, and then I got Adolf Klasner, my brother. Then I got another brother, Bernard Klasner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my brother Wolf Klasner. My sister-in-law was Jadzia Klasner. My nephew is Jozef Klasner. The other nephew is Samuel Klasner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what this is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This a document after the liberation. They give out to people what they want to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler doesn't give it to me because he was away. I have it-- a community where they write out, they had the papers printed with everything. They just write out for whom this is. And with this paper, I went to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what this is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- I got a tool when I was working in Brunnlitz in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose factory was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler's, Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of tool is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a tool what measurement in lathe machines. If you works, you have to measure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a tool you worked with in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shoes. I didn't have no shoes. They give me a pair of shoes like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you these shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you wear them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wear them over there, and then I came to Poland. I put away and hide today. I was hiding this. I wanted have it in memory.
